skill.name: jeo
skill.description: JEO coordinates AI agent workflows across planning, execution, UI review, browser verification, and cleanup. It includes setup scripts for Claude, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode integrations.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-agent planning, execution, UI feedback, and cleanup workflows.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis findings are largely true positives for invasive automation: the skill contains shell installers, Python and Bash hooks, networked local services, and writes to AI tool configuration under home directories. I found no evidence of credential exfiltration or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill should not be published without manual review and strong warnings because it can modify user projects and AI tool behavior.

high_findings.title: Remote installers and eval-backed command execution
high_findings.description: The installer runs commands through eval and includes remote install patterns such as a curl response piped to bash. This is dangerous because a network or upstream compromise can execute arbitrary code on the user system during setup.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static shell-execution and pipe-to-shell findings are confirmed by installer logic. The behavior is declared as setup automation, but the execution surface is still high risk.

high_findings.title: AI tool configuration and hook installation in home directories
high_findings.description: Setup scripts write to Claude, Codex, Gemini, and OpenCode configuration paths under the user home directory. They register hooks and MCP servers that can run commands during AI tool events.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple setup scripts directly target hidden AI tool configuration files and install persistent hooks. This confirms the filesystem and external-command risk factors in a semantically meaningful way.

high_findings.title: Event hooks execute workflow scripts and poll localhost services
high_findings.description: Codex and Claude hooks react to AI tool events, execute local workflow scripts, and query a localhost annotation service. This can affect future AI sessions and expose local workflow data to any process serving the expected local endpoint.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hooks are explicit and bounded to local workflow states, but they still create persistent execution paths tied to AI tool lifecycle events.

high_findings.title: Project source mutation and package installation for UI annotation
high_findings.description: The agentation helper can install an npm package and rewrite React entry files to mount an annotation component. This can alter application source code and dependency trees without a narrow allowlist.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly installs dependencies and edits source files. The purpose is documented, but the behavior is high impact for marketplace users.

medium_findings.title: Prompt-like mode instruction is installed into Codex prompts
medium_findings.description: The Codex setup writes a prompt that tells the assistant it is operating in JEO mode and mandates workflow steps. This is not an audit-bypass prompt, but it can strongly steer future assistant behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious phrase is present and installed as a user prompt. It does not claim special marketplace authority or tell auditors to skip review, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: Local network services are required for plan and annotation flows
medium_findings.description: The skill probes and connects to localhost ports for plannotator and agentation workflows. Localhost use is expected, but another local process could spoof responses or receive workflow data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The networking is local and aligned with the skill purpose. The risk remains meaningful because hooks trust localhost service behavior.

medium_findings.title: Forced worktree deletion guidance
medium_findings.description: The flow reference recommends forced worktree removal and recursive directory deletion during troubleshooting. This is documentation rather than executed code, but it can cause data loss if followed incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static recursive-delete hit is confirmed in documentation. It is not automatic execution, which lowers severity.

low_findings.title: Weak hash findings are non-security session identifiers
low_findings.description: Several weak-crypto static findings relate to MD5 use for deterministic temporary directory names. I did not find evidence that these hashes protect secrets or validate integrity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses MD5 to derive a short local session key from the current directory. That is weak for security, but this context appears non-cryptographic.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are mostly documented install or localhost endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are present in install instructions, local annotation endpoints, and tool configuration. They are not evidence of exfiltration by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and related to declared integrations. They still contribute to the overall risk because setup automation can contact external and local services.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance is mostly prerequisite and status checking
low_findings.description: Commands such as command detection, git root lookup, and settings inspection are used to check prerequisites and workflow state. No evidence found that these checks are used for stealth or evasion.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reconnaissance patterns are real, but the surrounding code shows diagnostic and path-discovery use. This is a low-risk true positive in context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe remote script to shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer can execute a remote plannotator install script through bash, which is unsafe without pinning or verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct pipe-to-shell installer pattern. It is a known dangerous pattern even when used for legitimate installation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent AI notification hook dispatcher
dangerous_patterns.description: The Codex setup creates and registers a dispatcher hook that runs registered Python hook files on notification events.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook dispatcher is explicitly written to disk and designed to execute local hook scripts. This is high-impact persistent automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden configuration file mutation
dangerous_patterns.description: Setup scripts create backups and write hidden AI tool configuration files in home directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly references and writes hidden configuration paths. This confirms static filesystem findings with clear context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated React entrypoint rewrite
dangerous_patterns.description: The agentation helper rewrites framework entry files to add a development annotation component.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code opens an entry file and writes modified content. The modification is purposeful, but it affects user source code.

content.value_statement: Complex agent workflows need planning, approval gates, browser checks, and cleanup across several tools. JEO provides one workflow layer for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode.
actual_capabilities: Creates a JEO workflow state model for plan, execute, verify, annotation, and cleanup phases.
Documents a mandatory plannotator plan review loop before implementation work proceeds.
Provides setup scripts for Claude, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode integrations.
Registers local agentation MCP and annotation workflows for UI feedback loops.
Includes browser verification guidance using agent-browser and playwriter tooling.
Includes worktree cleanup and status-check scripts for workflow maintenance.
limitations: Setup scripts can modify home-directory AI tool configuration and install hooks.
Several workflows require external CLIs, npm packages, and local services.
The annotation and plan review loops depend on localhost ports and local browser access.
This skill should be tested in a disposable workspace before use on important projects.

use_cases.title: Coordinate frontend implementation reviews
use_cases.description: Plan a UI task, implement it with an agent workflow, verify browser behavior, and process visual annotations.

use_cases.title: Enforce planning gates before execution
use_cases.description: Require a reviewed plan before multi-agent execution starts, then track approval state during the workflow.

use_cases.title: Evaluate cross-tool agent integrations
use_cases.description: Test how Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode can share orchestration conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Start a reviewed implementation plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use JEO to create a plan for this task. Show the plan, list risks, and wait for approval before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Run a UI verification loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Use JEO for this UI change. Plan the work, implement it, run browser verification, and summarize any visual issues found.

prompt_templates.title: Process annotation feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the JEO annotation workflow after I submit UI annotations. Acknowledge each item, apply fixes, and report resolved items.

prompt_templates.title: Audit setup before enabling hooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the JEO setup steps for my environment. Identify files that will change, commands that will run, and approval checkpoints before setup.

output_examples.input: Use JEO to plan a dashboard filter redesign.
output_examples.output: A plan summary with objectives, affected files, risks, acceptance criteria, and a clear approval request before coding starts.

output_examples.input: Run JEO browser verification for the updated checkout page.
output_examples.output: A verification report covering the tested URL, visible UI state, interaction results, defects found, and next fixes.

output_examples.input: Process submitted annotation feedback with JEO.
output_examples.output: A triage summary listing acknowledged comments, completed changes, unresolved questions, and the final annotation status.

best_practices: Run setup scripts with dry-run options first and review every file they will change.
Use a disposable workspace when testing hooks, MCP servers, and automatic package installation.
Stop local services after use and review generated AI tool configuration before future sessions.
anti_patterns: Do not run the full installer on a primary workstation without reviewing hook changes.
Do not allow automatic package installs in repositories with strict dependency controls.
Do not treat local annotation data as trusted input without human review.

faq.question: What does JEO do?
faq.answer: It coordinates planning, execution, UI annotation, browser verification, and cleanup across multiple AI coding tools.

faq.question: Which tools does it target?
faq.answer: The skill includes instructions and setup scripts for Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode.

faq.question: Does it install software?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts can call npm, npx, bun, plannotator, and other setup commands depending on selected options.

faq.question: Does it modify existing configuration?
faq.answer: Yes. Setup scripts can write hidden configuration files under home directories and register persistent hooks.

faq.question: Is it safe for production workstations?
faq.answer: Not without review. The audit rates it high risk because it can execute commands, edit source files, and install hooks.

faq.question: How should I evaluate it safely?
faq.answer: Use a disposable project, run dry-run modes, inspect hook changes, and keep local services isolated to localhost.
